pros: Outstanding sensitivity -- closest to climbing barefoot, uniform pressure distribution on holds, surprisingly durable for its thin rubber
cons: Expensive, forces you to confront bad footwork habits, not for beginners, limited on pure edging terrain
fit_notes: Aggressive downturn with a precise fit. Size snug -- loose fit ruins the No-Edge concept. Best for narrow-to-medium feet
best_for: Advanced climbers, high-friction slab and face climbing, sending burns

pros: Top-five out-of-the-box performance, heel is precise and tight for heel hooks, toe fits small pockets with ease
cons: Very specialized -- won't replace a traditional edging shoe, price is premium, not suitable for crack climbing
fit_notes: Snug fit is essential. The shoe stretches minimally. Half to full size down from street shoe recommended
best_for: Technical face climbing, competition routes, smearing-intensive problems

pros: Near-barefoot sensitivity with No-Edge technology, excellent smearing, Vibram XS Grip2 rubber is ultra-sticky, breaks in quickly
cons: Steep learning curve for technique, limited traditional edging ability, specialized shoe that won't be your only pair
fit_notes: Must fit snugly for No-Edge to work. Molds to foot after a few sessions. Not forgiving if sized too large
best_for: Experienced climbers seeking maximum sensitivity, smearing-heavy routes, granite slab

pros: Stiff sole paired with moderate downturn makes it an edging machine, comfortable enough for all-day cragging, jams well in thin cracks
cons: Not aggressive enough for steep bouldering, lace system slower for quick transitions
fit_notes: Generous fit compared to most La Sportiva performance shoes. Lined leather limits stretch. Size down 0.5 from street for performance
best_for: One-shoe quiver for outdoor climbing, technical trad routes, face climbing

pros: Incredible value as a do-everything shoe, slight update in 2022 improved an already great design, resoleable
cons: Narrow toe profile may not suit wide feet, less sensitivity than softer shoes for indoor climbing
fit_notes: Available in men's and women's lasts. Women's version has a narrower heel and adjusted volume. Comfortable for long pitches
best_for: Outdoor sport and trad climbing, crack climbing, multi-pitch adventures

pros: Excellent edging with Vibram XS Edge rubber, versatile from crack climbing to face routes, comfortable straight out of the box
cons: Laces take longer to adjust than velcro, downturned design can make wider cracks uncomfortable
fit_notes: Lined leather feels like a sock, padded tongue adds comfort. Laces allow micro-adjustments for any foot width. True to La Sportiva size
best_for: All-round trad and sport climbing, multi-pitch routes, long days at the crag

pros: Best gym climbing shoe according to editors, XS Grip2 rubber sticks to everything, minimalist design reduces weight and bulk
cons: Very little support on edgy slabs and technical outdoor faces, not durable on rough outdoor rock, niche use case
fit_notes: Designed for indoor use primarily. Thin construction means precise sizing is critical. Updated 2024 version has improved heel cup
best_for: Indoor gym climbing, modern competition style, training sessions

pros: Closest thing to coating your foot in rubber, molds to both foot and foothold without coercion, lightest shoe on market
cons: Small edges are dicey with no midsole or rubber edge, learning curve to carry own weight without midsole support
fit_notes: Ultra-minimalist fit. Molds completely to foot shape. Size snug -- any looseness undermines the concept
best_for: Gym bouldering, training boards, steep indoor climbing

pros: Nearly unmatched sensitivity, No-Edge technology maximizes rubber-to-hold contact, incredibly lightweight at 195g, smearing confidence is exceptional
cons: Virtually no edging support, voluminous heel makes heel hooking difficult on some versions, requires technique recalibration
fit_notes: Slipper-on design for quick transitions. Fits like a rubber sock. 2024 update improved heel fit. Best for narrow-to-medium feet
best_for: Indoor training, steep gym bouldering, competition warm-ups

pros: Reliable performance on vertical to slightly overhanging terrain, bombproof durability, resoleable for extended life
cons: Toe box shape doesn't match most natural foot shapes, clunky feel on volumes and smeary holds
fit_notes: Size for a snug fit -- the stiff leather won't stretch much. Works best for Egyptian or squared-off toe profiles
best_for: Outdoor sport climbing, trad single-pitch, vertical face routes

pros: The Ferrari of edging shoes -- stands on edges you can barely see, durable construction, iconic heritage design
cons: Not sensitive or flexible enough to smear well, break-in period is longer than average, comfort is poor for all-day use
fit_notes: Velcro closure for quick on/off. Runs true to La Sportiva sizing. Expect pain during the first 5-10 sessions
best_for: Hard sport climbing, limestone crags, technical face climbing

pros: Top-tier edging performance in a league of its own, exceptional on pockets of all sizes, P3 platform holds aggressive shape for months
cons: Stiff and uncomfortable for extended wear, weak toe-hooking ability due to minimal toe rand, limited smearing capacity
fit_notes: Wide toe box tapers to an aggressive point. Stiff construction means longer break-in. Best for medium-to-wide forefeet
best_for: Technical sport climbing, pocket-heavy limestone, steep edging

pros: Outstanding heel hooks, combines favorite aspects of multiple La Sportiva models, great durability with minimal toe rubber wear
cons: 4mm XS Edge is Vibram's stiffest compound reducing feel, not ideal for pure slab climbing
fit_notes: More accommodating than most La Sportiva performance shoes. Fits medium-to-wide feet well. Size true to La Sportiva
best_for: All-round outdoor climbing, technical face routes, moderate bouldering

pros: Workhorse shoe with surprising comfort for its aggressive profile, edges and jams with equal precision, great value
cons: Less sensitivity than softer shoes for indoor climbing, not aggressive enough for hardest overhanging problems
fit_notes: Generous fit by La Sportiva standards. Velcro closure for quick transitions. Works well for Egyptian and Roman toe shapes
best_for: Versatile outdoor climbing, trad routes, sport cragging, gym training

pros: Top-tier edging with P3 system, versatile for sport, trad, and gym, Vibram XS Edge rubber is exceptionally durable
cons: P3 insert reduces rock sensitivity, stiff sole struggles on smeary slab, velcro has unclipped mid-climb for some users
fit_notes: Not as narrow as other La Sportiva shoes, accommodates wider feet. Can be sized slightly larger and still perform. Comfortable for its aggression level
best_for: Quiver-of-one for outdoor climbing, single-pitch sport and trad, gym sessions

pros: Impressive heel-hooking with dedicated rubber patch, excellent gym performance with sticky rubber, slipper convenience with velcro security
cons: Not ideal for wide feet, moderate break-in for unlined leather, not the most aggressive downturn available
fit_notes: Narrow last -- great for low-volume feet. Unlined leather stretches to mold. Single velcro strap adds adjustability over a pure slipper
best_for: Gym bouldering, sport climbing, climbers with narrow feet

pros: Best fit for narrow feet in La Sportiva's lineup, Vibram XS Edge rubber holds precision on small footholds, lightweight and sensitive
cons: Wide feet will find it restrictive, sole is thin so foot gets more of a workout, less support than stiffer models
fit_notes: Specifically designed for narrow feet. Size down 0.5-1 from street. Leather upper molds to foot over time
best_for: Narrow-footed climbers, indoor training, precision footwork practice

pros: Worthy successor to the Cobra, smart new features like improved toe rubber, head-height heel hooks become significantly easier
cons: Asymmetric fit takes getting used to, not the most durable on rough outdoor rock, thin sole can be tiring
fit_notes: Slipper-style with velcro strap. Fits narrow to medium feet. Unlined leather will stretch 0.25-0.5 size
best_for: Indoor climbing, sport bouldering, all-round gym shoe

pros: Most comfortable performance shoe in its class, split sole gives outstanding flexibility, versatile from slabs to steep terrain
cons: Softer construction means shorter lifespan, doesn't resole as well as other models, less precision on tiny edges
fit_notes: Comfortable out of the box with minimal break-in. Fits narrow to medium feet well. Velcro closure allows quick adjustments
best_for: All-round performance climbing, bouldering, sport routes

pros: Exceptional toe-hooking, great sensitivity on small holds, P3 platform retains shape over time, excellent smearing on slopey terrain
cons: Weaker edging on vertical faces, rubber wears faster than stiffer competitors, not ideal for long multi-pitch routes
fit_notes: Runs narrow, size down 0.5-1 from street shoe. Minimal break-in period. Best for low-to-medium volume feet
best_for: Steep bouldering, overhangs, gym climbing

pros: Vibram XS Grip2 rubber is ultra-sticky, aggressive downturn for power moves, reliable heel hook performance
cons: Can feel sloppy on pure slab terrain, heel cup may be too tight for wider feet
fit_notes: Best for narrow-to-medium feet. Wide-footed climbers should try before buying. About half size down from street
best_for: Powerful boulderers, 3D features, steep sport routes

pros: Blends precision, power, and sensitivity into one package, great for steep 3D features and volumes
cons: Severe asymmetry amplifies pain during crack jams, less durable than the classic Solution
fit_notes: Designed for competition fit -- tight and precise. Women's version available with adjusted last. Downsize 0.5-1 from street
best_for: Competition climbers, aggressive bouldering, indoor and outdoor steep terrain

pros: Best-in-class edging for an aggressive shoe, extra toe rubber for improved toe hooks, softer and more sensitive than original Solution
cons: Expensive, dramatic downturn causes pain during crack climbing, not meant for all-day comfort
fit_notes: Slimmer heel than original Solution, more sensitive fit. Size similarly to the Solution. Best for narrow-to-medium feet
best_for: Competition climbing, steep bouldering, hard sport routes

pros: Softer 0.9mm LaSpo Flex midsole adds smearing ability the original lacked, excellent sensitivity, improved heel hook precision
cons: Rubber wears faster than the stiffer original Solution, premium price point, not versatile for multi-pitch or trad
fit_notes: Runs similar to Solution but with a slimmer, more anatomical heel. Break-in is quicker than the original
best_for: Indoor competition, steep outdoor bouldering, performance sport climbing

pros: Stiff toe box combined with flexible midfoot lets feet work like hands, great on steep pockets and overhangs
cons: Comfort is a mixed bag, heel bothers some testers on extended wear, not great on slabs
fit_notes: Tight initially but breaks in over time. Best for medium-width feet. The heel cup shape works for some foot types but not others
best_for: Hard bouldering, steep sport routes, competition climbing

pros: Legendary performance since 2007, used by world-class climbers (Ondra, Schubert), incredible precision and power on steep terrain
cons: Smearing is mediocre, fatiguing on long edgy pitches due to lack of full midsole, not suitable for crack climbing
fit_notes: Aggressive downturn with narrow toe. Sizing is tricky -- go snug but not painful. Leather stretches minimally
best_for: Single-pitch sport climbing, competition bouldering, overhanging routes

pros: Outstanding precision on micro-edges, excellent on pockets, P3 rand maintains downturn for life, ultra-sticky Vibram XS Grip rubber
cons: Strap degrades quickly from abrasion, heel cup can feel insensitive on some feet, significant break-in period required
fit_notes: Narrow toe box, runs small. Expect discomfort during break-in. Feet swell throughout the day making fit worse. Size for performance, not comfort
best_for: Steep sport climbing, bouldering, pocket-heavy limestone

pros: Can put feet virtually anywhere and stand up on granite, surprisingly good heel hooks, flat last bends for good smearing once broken in
cons: Not suitable for steep bouldering or competition climbing, fit can be challenging for some foot shapes
fit_notes: High-volume fit accommodates most foot shapes. Break-in period of 5-10 sessions. Leather softens and molds over time
best_for: Squamish-style granite, trad cragging, outdoor all-day climbing

pros: Redesigned version is more bombproof than ever, zero rand peeling after 20+ days, excellent smearing when broken in
cons: Not for steep gym climbing, heavy compared to performance slippers, takes time to break in properly
fit_notes: Lace-up with precise adjustment. Runs slightly large. Size down 0.5 for a snug fit or go true-to-size for all-day comfort
best_for: Traditional climbing, crack systems, long multi-pitch days

pros: The gold standard for trad climbing, incredible edging on granite, bomber heel hook stripe, leather upper withstands crack abuse
cons: Stiff flat sole struggles on overhanging terrain, clunky old-school feel, new ECO leather may stretch more and hold odor
fit_notes: Designed by Tommy Caldwell for all-day trad comfort. Size for comfort, not performance. Leather stretches after break-in
best_for: Trad climbing, granite cracks, multi-pitch routes, big wall climbing

pros: Ultra-soft design molds to foot and hold shapes, Vibram XS Grip2 is incredibly sticky, lightweight and agile feel
cons: Not the strongest for crack climbing, edges and thin footholds require more effort, durability is below average
fit_notes: Tight fit that loosens slightly with wear. Leather upper stretches minimally. Size for a performance fit, not comfort
best_for: Gym bouldering, modern competition problems, steep overhanging routes

pros: Unmatched sensitivity on modern gym holds, sticks to everything from tiny crimps to round slopers, exceptional toe-hooking weapon
cons: Soft rubber wears quickly on rough surfaces, not suited for long edging routes, painful break-in period of 10-15 sessions
fit_notes: Downsize 1.5-2 EU from street shoe. Wide feet may get hot spots over metatarsals. Very narrow last
best_for: Indoor competition climbing, steep bouldering, modern-style gym problems

pros: Best slab competition shoe on the market, conforms to overhung volumes and weird footprints perfectly, precise heel cup
cons: Limited durability on outdoor rock, minimal edge support, heel hooks can be painful on sharp holds
fit_notes: Aggressive fit -- expect discomfort initially. Designed for competition-tight sizing. Best for narrow-to-medium feet
best_for: Competition climbing, indoor bouldering, steep sport routes
